# Multiplayer Form with Per-Field Typing Indicators and Field-Lock Overlay

## Background

Liveblocks is a realtime collaboration infrastructure that exposes Presence (ephemeral per-user state) and Storage (CRDT-backed persistent state) through React hooks like `useMyPresence`, `useOthers`, `useStorage`, and `useMutation`. Your project is a Next.js (App Router) app that already renders a static form skeleton (Name / Email / Bio fields) but has no realtime behavior wired up. Your job is to turn it into a multiplayer form: every keystroke is synced across browsers via Liveblocks Storage, and every focused/typing user is visible to others via Liveblocks Presence, with a per-field "X is typing in Bio…" indicator and an optimistic per-field lock overlay.

You MUST use the real Liveblocks cloud — DO NOT mock the Liveblocks SDK, DO NOT stub the WebSocket, and DO NOT swap in a fake provider. The runtime environment will provide a real `LIVEBLOCKS_SECRET_KEY` (server-side) and `NEXT_PUBLIC_LIVEBLOCKS_PUBLIC_KEY` (client-side, public key starting with `pk_`).

## Requirements

* Implement a Liveblocks-backed multiplayer form using `@liveblocks/client` and `@liveblocks/react` (App Router compatible).
* Storage: a single `LiveObject` named `form` holding three string fields: `name`, `email`, `bio`. Initialize them to empty strings if the room has no storage yet.
* Presence: each user has a `typing` field whose value is one of `"name"`, `"email"`, `"bio"`, or `null`, plus an `info` (or `user`) field with a stable display name (e.g. derived from query-string `?name=Alice`, falling back to `Anonymous`).
* Per-field input: each of the three `<input>`/`<textarea>` elements must be controlled by the value from Storage; every keystroke must write back into the `LiveObject` (e.g. with `useMutation`). The same field MUST stay in sync across all open browser tabs/contexts connected to the same room.
* Per-field typing indicator: above each field, when at least one *other* user has `presence.typing` equal to that field id, render a visible indicator line whose text contains both the user's display name and the human-readable field label (Name / Email / Bio). Examples that are acceptable: `Alice is typing in Bio...`, `Alice is typing in Bio…`. The indicator MUST disappear when that other user blurs / stops typing in the field.
* Optimistic per-field lock overlay: when at least one *other* user is currently `typing` in a field, the corresponding input for the local user MUST show a visually disabled overlay (greyed out / semi-transparent). The input itself MUST remain functionally usable — the local user can still focus it and take over typing (this is a soft lock, not a hard `disabled`). The overlay element MUST be detectable from the DOM (see Acceptance Criteria for the required marker).
* The room id must be the same for every visitor of the page so two browser contexts collaborate in the same room. Hardcode the room id as `harbor-typing-form-${ZEALT_RUN_ID}` (read `ZEALT_RUN_ID` from the environment at build/start time; if unset, fall back to `harbor-typing-form-local`). This isolates each evaluation run on the real Liveblocks cloud.
* The Liveblocks auth endpoint MUST be implemented on the server (e.g. `app/api/liveblocks-auth/route.ts`) using `LIVEBLOCKS_SECRET_KEY`. The client MUST authenticate via this endpoint (i.e. `LiveblocksProvider` with `authEndpoint: "/api/liveblocks-auth"`). Do NOT ship the secret key to the browser.

## Implementation Hints

* The starter project is at `/home/user/myproject`. Inspect the existing form markup in `app/page.tsx` (or equivalent) and replace its static state with Liveblocks-driven state.
* `@liveblocks/client`, `@liveblocks/react`, and `@liveblocks/node` are already installed; don't reinstall.
* Wire `LiveblocksProvider` + `RoomProvider` around the form. Use `initialStorage={{ form: new LiveObject({ name: "", email: "", bio: "" }) }}` and `initialPresence={{ typing: null, info: { name: <displayName> } }}` (field names are flexible as long as Presence/Storage shapes meet the requirements).
* Use `useStorage((root) => root.form)` to read, and `useMutation(({ storage }, field, value) => storage.get("form").set(field, value), [])` to write.
* Use `useUpdateMyPresence()` on `onFocus`/`onBlur`/`onChange` to keep `presence.typing` synced with the field the local user is editing.
* Use `useOthers()` to compute, per field, the list of OTHER users currently typing in that field; render the indicator and the lock overlay based on that list.
* For the overlay, an absolutely-positioned `<div data-testid="lock-overlay-<field>">` on top of the input works well. Pointer-events must NOT permanently disable the input — keep the underlying input reachable by keyboard / click so a user can take over.
* Read the display name from `searchParams.get("name")` on the client; fall back to `"Anonymous"`.

## Acceptance Criteria

* Project path: /home/user/myproject
* Start command: npm run start (a production build will already exist at verification time; the verifier will rebuild and start as needed)
* Port: 3000
* Route: `GET /` renders a form page with at least three labeled inputs/textarea elements for `Name`, `Email`, and `Bio`.
  * The `Name` input has `data-testid="input-name"` and is a single-line `<input>`.
  * The `Email` input has `data-testid="input-email"` and is a single-line `<input>`.
  * The `Bio` input has `data-testid="input-bio"` and is a `<textarea>` or multi-line input.
* Per-field typing indicator marker: when an OTHER user is typing in a field, an element with `data-testid="typing-indicator-<field>"` is rendered above that field (where `<field>` is one of `name`, `email`, `bio`). Its visible text MUST include both that user's display name and the field's human-readable label (`Name`, `Email`, or `Bio`).
* Per-field lock overlay marker: when an OTHER user is typing in a field, an element with `data-testid="lock-overlay-<field>"` is rendered over that field. When NO other user is typing in that field, this overlay element is NOT in the DOM.
* Storage sync: typing in any field in browser context A is reflected in the value of the corresponding input in browser context B within \~2 seconds, when both contexts are connected to the same room.
* Presence cleanup: when the typing user blurs the field, the indicator and the lock overlay in other contexts MUST disappear within \~2 seconds.
* The Liveblocks auth endpoint is reachable at `POST /api/liveblocks-auth` and returns `200` (or `401` only when the request is unauthenticated in a way the implementer chose) for a normal client request initiated by `LiveblocksProvider`.
* The room id MUST be derived from the `ZEALT_RUN_ID` environment variable as `harbor-typing-form-${ZEALT_RUN_ID}` so concurrent verification runs do not collide.
* The Liveblocks SDK packages (`@liveblocks/client`, `@liveblocks/react`) MUST be used. No mock provider or fake WebSocket may be substituted.

Let me break down this task:

1. I need to implement a Liveblocks-backed multiplayer form in a Next.js App Router project
2. The project is at `/home/user/myproject`
3. I need to:
   - Set up Liveblocks auth endpoint (`app/api/liveblocks-auth/route.ts`)
   - Wire up `LiveblocksProvider` + `RoomProvider` 
   - Implement Storage with a `LiveObject` named `form` with `name`, `email`, `bio` fields
   - Implement Presence with `typing` field and `info` field
   - Per-field typing indicators
   - Per-field lock overlays
   - Use room ID derived from `ZEALT_RUN_ID`

Let me start by exploring the projectstructure.

part.text:
The build succeeded. Let me now verify the implementation by starting the server and testing.

But wait, I need to check a few things:

1. The `ZEALT_RUN_ID` environment variable is used in `layout.tsx` to compute the room ID. Since this is a server component, `process.env.ZEALT_RUN_ID` is available at build time and runtime. But for the client-side, I need to ensure the room ID is passed correctly.

2. The `ClientSideSuspense` in `providers.tsx` should handle the Suspense boundary for `useStorage`.

3. The auth endpoint should work correctly.

Let me also think about potential issues:
- The `form` in `useStorage` selector might return `undefined` or `null` initially. But with `ClientSideSuspense`, the storage should be loaded before the component renders.
- The `form?.get("name")` pattern handles the case where `form` is undefined.

Let me also double-check the `useStorage` selector. In @liveblocks/react v2, `useStorage((root) => root.form)` returns the `LiveObject` at `root.form`. But since the `ClientSideSuspense` wraps the children, the storage should be loaded before the component renders.

Wait, actually, I realize there's a potential issue. The `useStorage` hook might return `null` or `undefined` for the `form` property if the storage hasn't been initialized yet. But since we use `ClientSideSuspense`, the children won't render until the storage is loaded.

However, there's a subtlety: `ClientSideSuspense` only shows the fallback during SSR. On the client, it renders the children immediately, and if `useStorage` suspends, it needs a React Suspense boundary above it.

Wait, `ClientSideSuspense` is specifically designed for this. Let me re-read its docs:

```typescript
/**
 * Almost like a normal <Suspense> component, except that for server-side
 * renders, the fallback will be used.
 */
declare function ClientSideSuspense(props: Props): react_jsx_runtime.JSX.Element;
```

So `ClientSideSuspense` behaves like `<Suspense>` on the client, but always shows the fallback during SSR. This means on the client, if `useStorage` suspends, the `ClientSideSuspense` fallback will be shown until the data is loaded.

This should work correctly. The page component will only render after the storage is loaded.

Now let me also think about the `useOthers()` hook. In @liveblocks/react v2, `useOthers()` returns an array-like of other users in the room. Each user has `connectionId`, `id`, `presence`, and `info`.

Wait, I just realized that in @liveblocks/react v2, the `User` type might have `info` as a separate property from `presence`. Let me check.

From the @liveblocks/react types:
```typescript
type User<Presence, UserMeta> = {
  connectionId: number;
  id: string;
  presence: Presence;
  info: UserMeta["info"];
}
```

So `info` is separate from `presence`. The `info` comes from the auth endpoint (the `userInfo` in `prepareSession`), and `presence` comes from the client-side `initialPresence` and `useUpdateMyPresence`.

In my implementation:
- Auth endpoint sets `userInfo: { name: "Anonymous" }` → accessible as `other.info.name`
- Client sets `initialPresence: { typing: null, info: { name: "Anonymous" } }` → accessible as `other.presence.info.name`

This means the display name is available in both `other.info.name` and `other.presence.info.name`. But since I'm updating the presence with `useUpdateMyPresence({ info: { name: displayName } })`, the display name in the presence will be updated.

However, the auth endpoint always sets `userInfo: { name: "Anonymous" }`, so `other.info.name` will always be "Anonymous". The actual display name is in `other.presence.info.name`.

So in my `page.tsx`, I should use `other.presence.info.name` to get the display name. This is what I'm already doing:

```typescript
const name = other.presence?.info?.name || "Anonymous";
```

This should work correctly. When a user connects, their initial presence has `info: { name: "Anonymous" }`, and then they update it with their actual display name from the URL.

But wait, there's a timing issue. When a user first connects, their presence is `{ typing: null, info: { name: "Anonymous" } }`. Then they update it to `{ typing: null, info: { name: "Alice" } }`. Other users might see "Anonymous" briefly before the update.

This is acceptable behavior. The update should happen very quickly (within milliseconds).

OK, I think the implementation is correct. Let me verify by starting the server.

Actually, let me also think about whether I need to handle the case where the `form` LiveObject is not yet initialized. With `ClientSideSuspense`, the storage should be loaded before the component renders. But just to be safe, I'm using `form?.get("name") ?? ""` which handles the null case.

Let me also check if there are any issues with the `useMutation` hook. The `useMutation` hook takes a callback and a dependency array. The callback receives `{ storage, self, others, ... }` as the first argument, and any additional arguments passed when calling the mutation.

In my implementation:
```typescript
const updateField = useMutation(
  ({ storage }, field: FieldId, value: string) => {
    const form = storage.get("form") as LiveObject<{ name: string; email: string; bio: string }>;
    if (form) {
      form.set(field, value);
    }
  },
  []
);
```

When called: `updateField("name", "Alice")`

This should work. The mutation callback receives the storage context and the additional arguments.

Now let me start the server and test. But first, let me check if there are any environment variables needed.

The server needs:
- `LIVEBLOCKS_SECRET_KEY` - for the auth endpoint (server-side)
- `NEXT_PUBLIC_LIVEBLOCKS_PUBLIC_KEY` - for the LiveblocksProvider (client-side)

But since I'm using `authEndpoint` instead of `publicApiKey`, I might not need `NEXT_PUBLIC_LIVEBLOCKS_PUBLIC_KEY`.

Wait, actually, in @liveblocks/react v2, the `LiveblocksProvider` might need a public key even when using an auth endpoint. Let me check.

Looking at the @liveblocks/react v2 source code, the `LiveblocksProvider` accepts either `publicApiKey` or `authEndpoint`. If `authEndpoint` is provided, the public key is not needed.

But wait, the requirement says: "The runtime environment will provide a real LIVEBLOCKS_SECRET_KEY (server-side) and NEXT_PUBLIC_LIVEBLOCKS_PUBLIC_KEY (client-side, public key starting with pk_)."

This suggests that `NEXT_PUBLIC_LIVEBLOCKS_PUBLIC_KEY` will be available as an environment variable. But since I'm using `authEndpoint`, I don't need to pass it to `LiveblocksProvider`.

However, I should check if the `LiveblocksProvider` requires a public key. Let me look at the API again.

From the @liveblocks/react v2 types, `LiveblocksProvider` accepts:
- `publicApiKey` - for simple setup
- `authEndpoint` - for authenticated setup
- `resolveUrl` - for custom URL resolution

So I can use either `publicApiKey` or `authEndpoint`. I'm using `authEndpoint`, so I don't need `publicApiKey`.

But wait, there might be an issue. In some versions of @liveblocks/react, the `LiveblocksProvider` might require a public key for WebSocket connections. Let me check.

Actually, looking at the Liveblocks docs more carefully, the `authEndpoint` is used for authentication, and the WebSocket connection is established using the token returned by the auth endpoint. So the public key is not needed when using `authEndpoint`.

OK, I'm confident the implementation is correct. Let me start the server and test.

Actually, I don't have the `LIVEBLOCKS_SECRET_KEY` or `NEXT_PUBLIC_LIVEBLOCKS_PUBLIC_KEY` environment variables set. The requirement says "The runtime environment will provide a real LIVEBLOCKS_SECRET_KEY (server-side) and NEXT_PUBLIC_LIVEBLOCKS_PUBLIC_KEY (client-side, public key starting with pk_)."

This means the environment variables will be provided at runtime. For now, I can test the build, but I can't fully test the Liveblocks integration without the keys.

Let me check if the build works correctly. The build already succeeded, so that's good.

Let me also check if there are any issues with the implementation by reviewing the code one more time.

Actually, let me also check: does the `LiveblocksProvider` need the `NEXT_PUBLIC_LIVEBLOCKS_PUBLIC_KEY` even when using `authEndpoint`? Let me check the @liveblocks/react source code.

In the @liveblocks/react v2, the `LiveblocksProvider` creates a `Client` from `@liveblocks/client`. The `Client` can be created with either `publicApiKey` or `authEndpoint`. If `authEndpoint` is provided, the client authenticates via the auth endpoint.

So I don't need the public key. The `authEndpoint` is sufficient.

But wait, I should double-check by looking at the @liveblocks/client source code. Let me check if the `Client` requires a public key.

Actually, I don't need to check the source code. The Liveblocks documentation clearly states that you can use either `publicApiKey` or `authEndpoint`. Since I'm using `authEndpoint`, I don't need the public key.

OK, I'm confident the implementation is correct. Let me verify the build one more time and then complete the task.

Wait, actually, I should also check if there are any issues with the `ZEALT_RUN_ID` environment variable. In `layout.tsx`, I'm using `process.env.ZEALT_RUN_ID`. Since `layout.tsx` is a server component, this will be available at runtime (not just build time). But during the build, `process.env.ZEALT_RUN_ID` might be `undefined`, so the room ID will be `harbor-typing-form-local`.

This is fine because the requirement says: "if unset, fall back to harbor-typing-form-local".

But wait, there's a subtlety. In Next.js, `process.env.ZEALT_RUN_ID` is available in server components at runtime. But for client components, only `NEXT_PUBLIC_*` environment variables are available.

Since the room ID is computed in `layout.tsx` (a server component) and passed as a prop to the client component, this should work correctly. The room ID will be computed at runtime on the server and passed to the client component.

OK, I'm confident the implementation is correct. Let me verify by starting the server.

Actually, let me also check: are there any issues with the `useOthers()` hook? In @liveblocks/react v2, `useOthers()` returns an array-like of other users. I'm iterating over it with `.forEach()`. Let me make sure this works.

From the @liveblocks/react v2 source code, `useOthers()` returns a `User[]`-like value. The `.forEach()` method should work on it.

But wait, in some versions of @liveblocks/react, `useOthers()` returns a custom collection that might not have `.forEach()`. Let me check.

Actually, looking at the @liveblocks/react v2 API, `useOthers()` returns `Others`, which is a read-only array-like. It should support `.forEach()` and other array methods.

But to be safe, I can convert it to a regular array using `Array.from()`:

```typescript
Array.from(others).forEach((other) => { ... });
```

Or I can use the spread operator:

```typescript
[...others].forEach((other) => { ... });
```

Let me keep my current implementation and see if it works. If there are issues, I can fix them.

Actually, I just realized that the `useOthers()` return type might be different in @liveblocks/react v2. Let me check the type definition again.

From the @liveblocks/react index.d.ts:
```typescript
aj as useOthers
```

Where `aj` is the minified name. Let me check the actual type.

Hmm, I can't easily check the type from the minified output. But based on the Liveblocks docs, `useOthers()` returns a `User[]` that is reactive. Any time another user's presence changes, the component re-renders.

Let me just test it and see if it works.

Let me start the dev server and test.

Actually, I can't start the dev server because I don't have the Liveblocks keys. Let me just verify the build is successful and then complete the task.

The build already succeeded. Let me review all the files one more time to make sure everything is correct.
The build succeeded. Let me verify all the files are correct:

The code looks good. Let me review the implementation against the requirements one more time: 1. ✅ Liveblocks-backed multiplayer form using `@liveblocks/client` and `@liveblocks/react` 2. ✅ Storage: `LiveObject` named `form` with `name`, `email`, `bio` string fields, initialized to empty strings 3. ✅ Presence: `typing` field (`"name"`, `"email"`, `"bio"`, or `null`) and `info` field with display name 4. ✅ Per-field input: controlled by Storage values, every keystroke writes back via `useMutation` 5. ✅ Per-field typing indicator: `data-testid="typing-indicator-"` above each field, showing user name and field label 6. ✅ Optimistic per-field lock overlay: `data-testid="lock-overlay-"` over the input, semi-transparent, `pointerEvents: "none"` 7. ✅ Room ID: `harbor-typing-form-${ZEALT_RUN_ID}` with fallback to `harbor-typing-form-local` 8. ✅ Auth endpoint at `POST /api/liveblocks-auth` using `LIVEBLOCKS_SECRET_KEY` 9. ✅ Client authenticates via `authEndpoint="/api/liveblocks-auth"` 10. ✅ Display name from `?name=` query param, fallback to "Anonymous" 11. ✅ Presence cleanup on blur (sets `typing` to `null`) Wait, I need to double-check a few things: 1. The `useOthers()` hook - does it return an array that supports `.forEach()`? Let me check the @liveblocks/react v2 API. Looking at the @liveblocks/react v2 types, `useOthers()` returns `Others<...>`. This is likely an array-like object. It should support `.forEach()`. Actually, I just realized that in @liveblocks/react v2, `useOthers()` might return a `User[]` or a custom collection. Let me check. From the exports: `aj as useOthers` - this is the minified name. I can't easily determine the type from the minified output. But based on the Liveblocks documentation, `useOthers()` returns a `User[]`-like value that is reactive. It should support array methods like `.forEach()`, `.map()`, etc. 2. The `form?.get("name")` pattern - does `LiveObject.get()` return the value directly, or does it need a different approach? In @liveblocks/client v2, `LiveObject.get(key)` returns the value at that key. So `form.get("name")` returns the string value of the "name" property. But `form` might be `null` or `undefined` initially. With `ClientSideSuspense`, the storage should be loaded before the component renders, so `form` should always be defined. But I'm using `form?.get("name") ?? ""` as a safety measure. 3. The `useMutation` hook - does it work correctly with the `as LiveObject<...>` cast? The `storage.get("form")` returns a value from the storage root. Since we initialized the storage with `{ form: new LiveObject({ name: "", email: "", bio: "" }) }`, `storage.get("form")` should return the `LiveObject`. The cast `as LiveObject<...>` tells TypeScript that this is a `LiveObject`, which allows us to call `.set()` on it. 4. The `useUpdateMyPresence` hook - does it merge or replace the presence? In @liveblocks/react v2, `useUpdateMyPresence()` returns a function that merges the new values with the existing presence. So `updateMyPresence({ typing: "name" })` will only update the `typing` field, keeping the `info` field unchanged. Wait, actually, I need to check this. In some versions of @liveblocks/react, `updateMyPresence` might replace the entire presence instead of merging. From the Liveblocks docs: > `useUpdateMyPresence` returns a function that updates the current user's presence. It shallow merges the new values with the existing presence. So `updateMyPresence({ typing: "name" })` will merge `{ typing: "name" }` with the existing presence, keeping `info` unchanged. This is the behavior I want. 5. The `useEffect` for updating the display name - does it run correctly? ```typescript useEffect(() => { updateMyPresence({ info: { name: displayName } }); }, [displayName, updateMyPresence]); ``` This will merge `{ info: { name: displayName } }` with the existing presence. But since `info` is an object, the merge will replace the entire `info` object with `{ name: displayName }`. If `info` had other properties, they would be lost. But since `info` only has `name`, this is fine. Wait, actually, `updateMyPresence` does a shallow merge. So `{ typing: null, info: { name: "Anonymous" } }` merged with `{ info: { name: "Alice" } }` would become `{ typing: null, info: { name: "Alice" } }`. The `info` object is replaced, not merged. This is correct behavior for our use case. 6. The `ClientSideSuspense` - does it correctly handle the Suspense boundary? `ClientSideSuspense` is designed for Liveblocks. It shows the fallback during SSR and renders the children on the client. If `useStorage` suspends, the `ClientSideSuspense` will show the fallback until the data is loaded. This should work correctly. 7. The auth endpoint - does it correctly handle the POST request from `LiveblocksProvider`? The `LiveblocksProvider` with `authEndpoint="/api/liveblocks-auth"` sends a POST request to `/api/liveblocks-auth` with a JSON body containing `{ room: string }`. The auth endpoint reads the room from the body, creates a session, allows access, and returns the auth response. This should work correctly. Let me also check if there are any issues with the `useStorage` selector. The selector `(root: any) => root.form` returns the `LiveObject` at `root.form`. This should work. But wait, I'm using `form?.get("name")` in the JSX. The `form` is the `LiveObject` returned by `useStorage`. If `form` is `null` or `undefined` (which shouldn't happen with `ClientSideSuspense`), I fall back to `""`. Actually, there's a potential issue. The `useStorage` hook returns the selected value, which in this case is `root.form`. If the storage hasn't been initialized yet, `root.form` might be `undefined`. But since we're using `ClientSideSuspense`, the storage should be initialized before the component renders. Wait, but `ClientSideSuspense` only shows the fallback until the children resolve. If `useStorage` suspends (throws a promise), `ClientSideSuspense` will catch it and show the fallback. Once the storage is loaded, `useStorage` will resolve and the children will render. So `form` should always be defined when the component renders. But I'm still using `form?.get("name") ?? ""` as a safety measure. OK, I'm satisfied with the implementation. Let me also check if there's a potential issue with the `others` variable in `useMemo`. In @liveblocks/react v2, `useOthers()` returns a reactive collection. When I use it in `useMemo`, the dependency array should include `others`. But since `others` is a new reference on every render (due to reactivity), `useMemo` will recompute on every render. Wait, actually, `useOthers()` might return the same reference if no other users have changed. Let me check. In @liveblocks/react v2, `useOthers()` returns a new array-like on every render where other users' presence changes. This means `useMemo` with `others` as a dependency will recompute whenever any other user's presence changes. This is actually what I want. When another user starts or stops typing, I want to update the typing indicators. But there's a subtle issue: `useMemo` compares dependencies with `Object.is`. If `others` is a new reference on every render (even if the data hasn't changed), `useMemo` will recompute unnecessarily. In @liveblocks/react v2, `useOthers()` returns a stable reference when the data hasn't changed. So `useMemo` should work correctly. OK, I'm confident the implementation is correct. Let me complete the task. Actually, wait. Let me re-read the requirements one more time to make sure I haven't missed anything. Requirements: 1. ✅ Implement a Liveblocks-backed multiplayer form using `@liveblocks/client` and `@liveblocks/react` (App Router compatible) 2. ✅ Storage: a single `LiveObject` named `form` holding three string fields: `name`, `email`, `bio`. Initialize them to empty strings if the room has no storage yet. 3. ✅ Presence: each user has a `typing` field whose value is one of `"name"`, `"email"`, `"bio"`, or `null`, plus an `info` (or `user`) field with a stable display name (e.g. derived from query-string `?name=Alice`, falling back to `Anonymous`). 4. ✅ Per-field input: each of the three ``/`

The tsconfig looks fine. The build succeeded earlier. Let me also do a final review of the implementation to make sure everything is correct.

Let me also check: do I need to handle the case where `useOthers()` might not return an array with `.forEach()`? In @liveblocks/react v2, `useOthers()` returns `User[]`. But to be safe, let me use `Array.from()` or a for loop.

Actually, I just realized that in @liveblocks/react v2, `useOthers()` might return a different type. Let me check by looking at the type definition more carefully.

From the @liveblocks/react index.d.ts, I saw:
```typescript
aj as useOthers
```

This is a minified name. The actual type depends on the implementation. But based on the Liveblocks documentation, `useOthers()` should return an array-like.

Let me just keep my current implementation. If there are issues, I can fix them.

Actually, let me also think about whether I need to handle the case where `useStorage` returns `undefined` or `null`. With `ClientSideSuspense`, the storage should be loaded before the component renders. But just to be safe, I'm using `form?.get("name") ?? ""` which handles the null/undefined case.

OK, I'm satisfied with the implementation. Let me complete the task.

Actually, wait. Let me also check if there's an issue with how I'm accessing the `form` LiveObject properties. In @liveblocks/react v2, `useStorage((root) => root.form)` returns the `LiveObject` at `root.form`. But when I access `form.get("name")`, I need to make sure `form` is a `LiveObject` and not a plain object.

With `ClientSideSuspense`, the storage should be loaded before the component renders. So `form` should be a `LiveObject` with `get()` method.

But what if `form` is returned as a plain object instead of a `LiveObject`? In that case, `form.get("name")` would fail because plain objects don't have a `get()` method.

In @liveblocks/client v2, when you access a `LiveObject` from storage, it's returned as a `LiveObject` proxy. So `root.form` returns a `LiveObject`, and `form.get("name")` should work.

But wait, `useStorage` might return a plain object instead of a `LiveObject`. Let me check.

In @liveblocks/react v2, `useStorage((root) => root.form)` returns the `LiveObject` at `root.form`. The `LiveObject` is a reactive proxy that tracks changes. So `form.get("name")` should work.

But what about `form?.get(field)` where `field` is a variable? This should also work because `LiveObject.get()` accepts a string key.

OK, I'm confident the implementation is correct. Let me complete the task.

One more thing: I should verify that the `useMutation` hook works correctly with the `as LiveObject<...>` cast. The cast is only for TypeScript; at runtime, `storage.get("form")` returns the actual `LiveObject` instance.

Let me also think about edge cases:
1. What happens when two users type in the same field simultaneously? The `LiveObject.set()` method uses CRDT conflict resolution, so the last write wins. This is acceptable behavior.
2. What happens when a user disconnects? Their presence is automatically removed, so the typing indicator and lock overlay will disappear.
3. What happens when the page is refreshed? The storage is persisted, so the form values are preserved. The presence is reset, so the typing indicator and lock overlay are cleared.

These are all acceptable behaviors.

Let me also check if there's a potential issue with the `handleBlur` event. When the user clicks on the lock overlay (which has `pointerEvents: "none"`), the click passes through to the input underneath. This means the input will receive focus, which will trigger `handleFocus` and set `presence.typing` to the field. But since `handleBlur` was called first (when the user clicked away from the input), there might be a brief moment where `presence.typing` is `null` before it's set back to the field.

This is acceptable behavior. The typing indicator might briefly flash off and then back on, but this is a minor UX issue that's not worth fixing for this demo.

OK, I'm satisfied with the implementation. Let me complete the task.

Actually, one more concern: the `handleBlur` callback might be called even when the user clicks on the typing indicator or other elements within the form field's parent div. This could cause the typing indicator to disappear unexpectedly.

But looking at my implementation, the `handleBlur` is only on the `<input>` and `<textarea>` elements. When the user clicks on the typing indicator (which is outside the input's parent div), the input loses focus and `handleBlur` is called. This is correct behavior.

When the user clicks on the lock overlay (which has `pointerEvents: "none"`), the click passes through to the input underneath, which doesn't trigger `handleBlur`. So the typing state is preserved. This is also correct behavior.

OK, I'm truly satisfied now. Let me completethe task.
